question: [File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: rateLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that rateLimitBucket uses refillGreedy with capacity=requestsPerSecond=500 and refill=500 per second, by sending exactly 500 requests in rapid succession to exhaust the bucket, then waiting for refill and repeating, creating a sustained pattern that prevents legitimate fund transfer requests from ever getting through?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that restore() in ThrottleManagerImpl only adds tokens if scaleGas(gas) > 0, meaning if the gas value passed to restore() is <= GAS_SCALE_FACTOR (10000), no tokens are restored, and by crafting requests that trigger restore() with small gas values, the attacker can cause net token depletion over time and freeze fund operations?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the integer truncation in scaleGas() (Math.floorDiv(gas, 10000)) to consume more gas tokens than are restored, since the consumed amount is scaleGas(requestGas) but the restored amount is scaleGas(gasRemaining) where gasRemaining < requestGas, causing a systematic net loss of tokens in gasLimitBucket and eventually freezing all fund operations?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that gasLimitBucket is a singleton Spring bean initialized once at startup with a fixed capacity derived from throttleProperties.getGasPerSecond(), meaning any permanent depletion of the bucket (tokens consumed but not restored) accumulates across requests and eventually reaches zero, permanently freezing all fund-related contract calls?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user send a high-gas request that passes throttle() and consumes gasLimitBucket tokens, then trigger an IllegalStateException or IllegalArgumentException during EVM execution (which is caught and rethrown as MirrorEvmTransactionException), and if the finally block in doProcessCall() fails to call restoreGasToBucket() due to a secondary exception, the gas tokens are permanently lost and fund operations are frozen?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the estimate=true path in ContractExecutionService where restoreGasToBucket() is skipped (guarded by !estimate check in doProcessCall()), by sending estimate requests with high gas values that consume gasLimitBucket tokens but never restore them, permanently depleting the bucket and freezing fund operations?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that gasLimitBucket capacity is computed as Math.floorDiv(gasPerSecond, GAS_SCALE_FACTOR) at bean initialization time, and if gasPerSecond is set to a value just above GAS_SCALE_FACTOR (e.g., 10001), the bucket capacity is 1, meaning a single high-gas request permanently exhausts the bucket until the next second refill, freezing all concurrent fund operations?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that gasLimitBucket.tryConsume() in ThrottleManagerImpl.throttle() is called with scaleGas(request.getGas()), and if the request gas is exactly at a boundary where scaleGas() returns the full bucket capacity, a single request can consume all available tokens, blocking all other users' fund transfer calls for the entire refill window?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: rateLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that rateLimitBucket has no synchronization strategy (unlike gasLimitBucket which uses SynchronizationStrategy.SYNCHRONIZED), allowing concurrent threads to simultaneously read the bucket state as non-empty and all consume tokens, effectively bypassing the requestsPerSecond limit and flooding the gasLimitBucket with more requests than intended, permanently depleting it?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the gasLimitBucket bean is created with proxyBeanMethods=false in @Configuration, meaning no Spring proxy wraps the bean creation, and if the ThrottleProperties bean is modified concurrently during startup, the gasLimitBucket could be initialized with an incorrect capacity of 0, permanently blocking all fund operations?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user send requests with gas values that cause scaleGas() to return exactly the current gasLimitBucket token count, consuming all tokens in a single request, and since the bucket uses refillGreedy (not refillIntervally), the refill is gradual within the second window, meaning subsequent fund transfer requests are blocked for the remainder of the window?
